instruction.global: Throw two additional red dice into the scene, causing them to collide with the existing dice and eventually come to a rest scattered across the surface.
steps: Introduce two new red dice falling into the frame from the top and right, applying motion blur to indicate high velocity.
Show the falling dice impacting the white surface and colliding with the original dice, causing the left-most die to be pushed further back.
Bring all four dice to a complete stop, removing motion blur and positioning them in a scattered arrangement across the table.

explain: Two additional dice enter the frame from above, their rapid descent creating significant motion blur.
The new dice strike the surface and the existing objects, transferring kinetic energy and causing the dice to bounce and shift positions.
Friction and gravity dissipate the kinetic energy, allowing all four dice to settle into stable, stationary positions.
invariants: Red color and translucent material of the dice

instruction.global: Initiate and complete a chain reaction of falling dominoes from left to right until all tiles are resting on the surface.
steps: Tip the first domino on the left forward to strike the second one, creating motion blur as the first few tiles begin to fall.
Propagate the falling motion through the center of the row, showing the dominoes leaning into each other in a continuous wave.
Complete the sequence so that all dominoes have fallen and settled into a stable, overlapping resting position on the table.

explain: The first domino's potential energy is converted to kinetic energy, which is transferred to the next tile upon impact, overcoming its static equilibrium.
Gravity pulls the tilted dominoes downward while momentum carries the force forward through the line, maintaining the chain reaction.
The dominoes reach a state of mechanical equilibrium where they are supported by the surface and the friction between overlapping tiles.
invariants: Plain white background and surface texture
The specific pip patterns and colors on each individual domino tile

instruction.global: Show the dominoes falling in a chain reaction until the entire line has collapsed.
steps: Collapse the first few dominoes on the left so they lie flat on the surface.
Tilt the middle section of the domino line forward as the chain reaction continues.
Complete the chain reaction by knocking down the remaining upright dominoes on the right.

explain: Kinetic energy from the first falling dominoes is transferred to the next ones, causing them to tip over and settle on the ground.
Gravity pulls the tilted dominoes down as they are struck by the preceding ones, creating a wave of motion.
The momentum carries through to the end of the line, resulting in all dominoes resting in a fallen state.
invariants: The specific dot patterns and colors on each domino

instruction.global: Scatter a handful of almonds onto the surface so they fall, bounce, and come to rest.
steps: Introduce several almonds falling from the top of the frame towards the surface, casting soft shadows below them.
Depict the almonds bouncing and tumbling across the surface, with one almond entering the extreme foreground.
Bring all the almonds to a complete stop, scattered across the surface in their final resting positions.

explain: Gravity accelerates the almonds downwards from an external source towards the plane.
Elastic collisions with the surface cause the almonds to bounce and rotate mid-air, distributing them across the frame.
Kinetic energy is dissipated through friction and multiple impacts, resulting in a static state where all objects are at rest.
invariants: dark background with faint grid pattern

instruction.global: A marble rolls from the left foreground into the distant background, passing behind the central cluster of marbles.
steps: Introduce a large, blurry marble entering from the left side of the frame, showing motion blur.
Move the rolling marble into the background behind the central group, transitioning from motion blur to a soft bokeh effect.
Position the marble further into the distance along the wooden planks, increasing the depth-of-field blur as it recedes.

explain: The marble enters with high velocity, creating motion blur because its movement exceeds the camera's shutter speed.
As the marble moves away from the camera's focal plane, it loses sharpness due to the shallow depth of field.
The marble continues its trajectory into the background, appearing smaller and more out of focus due to perspective and lens optics.
invariants: The positions and orientations of the central cluster of marbles.
The texture, cracks, and grain of the wooden planks.
The lighting direction and shadow placement.
The static marbles in the far left and right background.

instruction.global: Knock down the pyramid of three blue bottles by throwing a ball at them from the left.
steps: Maintain the stable pyramid formation of the three blue bottles on the white table.
Introduce a patterned ball flying from the left side of the frame to strike the center of the bottle stack, causing it to tilt.
Remove the ball and depict the three bottles lying scattered and fallen on the table surface.

explain: The bottles remain in a state of static equilibrium, supported by the table and each other.
Kinetic energy from the moving ball is transferred to the bottles upon impact, overcoming their gravitational stability.
Gravity pulls the displaced bottles downward until they reach a new stable state resting horizontally on the table.
invariants: White table surface and plain background wall
The specific design, color, and number of the blue bottles

instruction.global: Strike the standing bowling pin with a blue bowling ball and then clear the lane using the mechanical sweep bar.
steps: Introduce a blue bowling ball striking the pin, causing the pin to fly to the right with significant motion blur.
Bring down a mechanical sweep bar from the top left to push the ball and pin off the lane.
Complete the sweep movement to leave the lane surface entirely empty.

explain: The bowling ball transfers kinetic energy to the pin upon impact, resulting in high-velocity projectile motion and directional blur.
A mechanical pinsetter sweep applies a constant lateral force to clear debris from the lane surface.
The lane returns to a state of rest once all objects are pushed into the pit and the sweep bar retracts.
invariants: Dark background structure of the pin deck

instruction.global: Knock down the miniature red bowling pins by rolling the small ball into them until they are all scattered across the table.
steps: Roll the ball into the left side of the pin formation, causing the first few pins to tip and fall.
Scatter the remaining standing pins as the ball and falling pins collide with them, spreading them across the white surface.
Allow the pins to settle into their final resting positions on the table as the ball rolls further away.

explain: Kinetic energy is transferred from the moving ball to the pins upon impact, overcoming their gravitational stability and causing them to tip.
A chain reaction occurs as falling pins strike adjacent pins, distributing the initial momentum throughout the group and knocking them over.
Friction between the pins and the table surface, combined with gravity, causes the objects to lose kinetic energy and come to a complete rest.
invariants: Red color and shape of the miniature pins
Presence of the small red and black ball
Consistent indoor lighting and soft shadows
Blurred background elements including the person

instruction.global: Release the rightmost ball of the Newton's cradle and allow the momentum to transfer back and forth until the balls come to a complete stop.
steps: Release the rightmost ball from the hand so it strikes the stationary balls, causing the leftmost ball to swing upward.
Swing the leftmost ball back down to strike the center balls, transferring momentum so the rightmost ball swings upward.
Allow the swinging motion to dampen until all five balls hang vertically and stationary in the center.

explain: The potential energy of the raised right ball converts to kinetic energy, which is transferred through elastic collisions through the center balls to the leftmost ball.
The leftmost ball's kinetic energy is transferred back through the stack, demonstrating conservation of momentum as the rightmost ball rises.
Energy is gradually lost to friction and air resistance, causing the amplitude of the swings to decrease until the system reaches equilibrium at rest.
invariants: Newton's cradle frame position and orientation

instruction.global: Perform a break shot on the racked billiard balls, causing them to scatter across the red felt table until only a few remain in the frame.
steps: Strike the apex of the racked balls with the cue ball, causing the formation to break and balls to begin moving outward with motion blur.
Scatter the balls further across the table surface, increasing the distance between them as they move towards the frame edges.
Remove the balls that have exited the frame or entered pockets, leaving only a few stationary balls scattered on the felt.

explain: Kinetic energy from the cue ball is transferred to the racked balls upon impact, overcoming static friction and initiating momentum in multiple directions.
The balls continue to travel across the low-friction felt surface, spreading out as they conserve momentum and undergo secondary collisions.
Friction and energy loss from collisions eventually cause the balls to slow down, with many exiting the field of view while others come to a complete stop.
invariants: Red felt texture and color of the pool table
Overhead warm lighting and shadow direction

instruction.global: Strike the white cue ball with the cue stick so it rolls toward the bottom left corner while the hand and stick exit the frame.
steps: Shift the hand and cue stick slightly as the player prepares the stroke.
Apply a force to the cue ball with the stick, making it roll toward the bottom left with visible motion blur.
Move the cue ball further along its trajectory and remove the hand and cue stick from the top left corner.

explain: The player adjusts their aim and prepares for the kinetic transfer.
The cue stick strikes the cue ball, converting potential energy into kinetic energy and causing motion across the felt.
The cue ball maintains its momentum while the player retracts the cue stick out of the camera's field of view.
invariants: Red felt texture and table rails

instruction.global: Add several small spheres to the scene by stacking one on the left and placing others near the central arch.
steps: Place a second white sphere directly on top of the existing white sphere on the left-most cube.
Place a white sphere on the floor surface in front of the wooden block under the pink arch.
Place a beige wooden sphere on the floor behind the white sphere that was just added.

explain: A new sphere is balanced on top of another, with gravity and contact friction maintaining the stack.
A sphere is introduced to the ground plane, resting stably on the flat surface.
An additional sphere is placed on the ground, creating depth through occlusion with the sphere in front of it.
invariants: Soft studio lighting and shadow direction
Positions and textures of all original geometric objects

instruction.global: A blue bowling ball rolls down the lane, strikes the pins, and the mechanical sweep bar descends to clear the lane.
steps: Introduce a blue bowling ball rolling from the right side of the lane toward the center of the pin formation.
Show the ball impacting the pins, causing the front and center pins to fly backward and to the sides with motion blur.
Remove the scattered pins and the ball, leaving one pin standing while lowering the orange 'STOP' sweep bar across the lane.

explain: The bowling ball possesses linear momentum as it rolls toward the target.
Kinetic energy is transferred from the ball to the pins during the collision, resulting in elastic and inelastic scattering of the pins.
The mechanical pinsetter clears the fallen pins from the deck and lowers a barrier to prevent further balls from being thrown during the reset.
invariants: Wooden lane texture and glossy reflections

instruction.global: A blue curling stone slides down the ice and knocks the stationary red stone out of the house, taking its position in the foreground.
steps: Introduce a blue-handled curling stone sliding from the background towards the red stone in the foreground.
Move the blue stone closer to the red stone, maintaining its path along the ice surface.
Replace the red stone with the blue stone at the center of the target after a collision, with the red stone being knocked out of the frame.

explain: A second curling stone is launched from the far end of the rink, sliding across the low-friction ice surface towards the target.
The blue stone continues its forward trajectory, gaining proximity to the stationary red stone due to its initial momentum.
An elastic collision occurs where the blue stone transfers its kinetic energy to the red stone, displacing it and coming to rest in its place.
invariants: Background elements including the blue pillars and the person in the distance
Ice surface texture and the painted red and blue circles
Lighting and shadows on the ice

instruction.global: Roll green apples across the blue floor from the left side of the frame while keeping the pedestals and stacked objects stationary.
steps: Introduce a green apple rolling into the frame from the left with visible motion blur.
Advance the first apple further left and add a second green apple rolling in from the same direction.
Remove the second apple and show the first apple stationary at the far left edge of the frame.

explain: A green apple enters the scene from the left with horizontal momentum, causing motion blur.
The first apple continues its path while a second apple enters the frame, demonstrating sequential motion.
The second apple exits the frame or is removed, leaving the first apple at rest near the left boundary.
invariants: White cube and sphere on the left
White cylinder and the stacked fruits (green apple and yellow quince) on the right

instruction.global: Introduce small white balls and have the large yellow ball fall from its balanced position on the cube to the floor.
steps: Add several small white balls scattered across the dark floor surface.
Shift the large yellow ball so it begins to roll off the edge of the tan cube while the white balls roll across the floor.
Place the large yellow ball at rest on the floor next to the tan cube, with most white balls having rolled out of view.

explain: Small white spheres are introduced as new dynamic elements in the scene.
The large yellow ball loses its equilibrium on the corner of the cube and begins to fall due to gravity.
The yellow ball completes its descent and reaches a stable state on the ground surface.
invariants: Positions and textures of the tan cube, yellow cube, white stick, and stone slabs.

instruction.global: Complete a full swing cycle of the Newton's cradle, starting from the right ball swinging in and ending with the right ball swinging back out after momentum transfers through the center.
steps: Move the rightmost ball to strike the center group and swing the leftmost ball out to the left.
Swing the leftmost ball back down from its peak position towards the center group.
Have the leftmost ball strike the center group and swing the rightmost ball out to the right.

explain: Elastic collision transfers kinetic energy from the right ball through the stationary balls to the leftmost ball, causing it to lift.
The leftmost ball reaches its maximum potential energy at the peak of its arc and begins its descent due to gravity.
The returning leftmost ball transfers its momentum back through the chain, launching the rightmost ball into the air again.
invariants: Plain blue background and supporting surface
The three central balls remain in their resting positions

instruction.global: Add a plastic water bottle to the left of the glass bottle and then tip the glass bottle over to the right in successive stages.
steps: Place a clear plastic water bottle with a blue cap vertically to the left of the glass bottle.
Tilt the glass bottle approximately 15 degrees to the right while keeping the water bottle stationary.
Increase the tilt of the glass bottle to approximately 45 degrees to the right, ensuring the base remains near its original contact point.

explain: A second object is introduced into the scene, requiring the generation of realistic shadows and reflections on the surface consistent with the existing lighting.
The glass bottle undergoes a rotational transformation around the right edge of its base, simulating the initial phase of tipping over.
The angular displacement of the glass bottle increases as gravity pulls its center of mass further away from the vertical equilibrium point.
invariants: Appearance and position of the plastic water bottle once added
Integrity of the glass bottle and its red cap

instruction.global: Scatter the central cluster of marbles across the wooden surface until they come to a rest.
steps: Disperse the central group of marbles, pushing them outward from the center point.
Continue the outward rolling motion of the marbles, with some moving toward the foreground and others toward the background.
Bring the marbles to a complete stop in their final scattered positions, ensuring they rest naturally on the wood grain.

explain: An initial force or collision causes the marbles to overcome static friction and begin rolling away from each other.
Kinetic energy carries the marbles across the surface, with their paths influenced by the texture of the wood.
Friction and loss of momentum cause the marbles to decelerate and settle into stable positions on the planks.
invariants: Shallow depth of field with blurred background
Rainbow color gradient lighting across the scene
Wooden plank texture and grain patterns
Internal patterns and transparency of the glass marbles

instruction.global: A blue curling stone slides in from the top, strikes the red stone on the left, knocking it out of the frame, and comes to rest near the center.
steps: Introduce a blue curling stone sliding from the top edge towards the center of the house.
Move the blue stone to collide with the red stone on the left, causing the red stone to shift towards the left edge.
Remove the red stone as it slides out of frame and settle the blue stone in its final position near the center.

explain: A blue curling stone is launched and slides across the low-friction ice surface towards the stationary red stones.
The blue stone undergoes an elastic collision with the red stone on the left, transferring its kinetic energy and momentum.
The struck red stone continues its trajectory out of the field of view while the blue stone decelerates due to ice friction and comes to a halt.
invariants: Ice surface texture and blue/white circular markings
Position and orientation of the red stone on the bottom right
Lighting and specular reflections on the ice
